summary: Gruber addresses the media frenzy surrounding Steve Jobs's visibly thin appearance at Apple's September 2008 iPod event, where columnists from Time and Newsweek focused more on Jobs's health than on product announcements. He acknowledges the speculation isn't unfounded given Jobs's 2004 cancer surgery and a reported second procedure, but points to medical explanations short of cancer recurrence — specifically complications from the Whipple procedure. Gruber criticizes Josh Quittner and Dan Lyons for dismissing the event as merely a vehicle to prove Jobs was alive, noting Apple has held identical September events every year since 2001. He argues that if Jobs were terminally ill and lying about it, Apple's board would be complicit in securities fraud, making that scenario implausible.
key_points: Medical explanations exist for Jobs's weight loss beyond cancer recurrence, including complications from the Whipple procedure requiring further intestinal surgery
Apple PR's claim that Jobs's gaunt appearance was from 'a common bug' was so implausible it fueled worse speculation — a self-inflicted credibility wound
Quittner and Lyons claiming the event was unusual ignores that Apple has held nearly identical September iPod events every year since 2001
If Jobs were terminally ill and lying, Apple's board would be complicit in securities fraud — a meaningful legal constraint on deception
The media critics were in a no-win setup: had Apple skipped the event, the same writers would have cited Jobs's absence as proof of illness
money_quotes: The speculation is not wholly unfounded. If Apple and Jobs do not wish to discuss the details of his health other than in the broadest of terms, that leaves the outside world with only a few cold hard facts, one of which is what our eyes tell us.
That seemed so Kremlin-esque, so clearly an insufficient explanation, that it's understandable that some people, including investors, jumped to the worst-case conclusion.
The obvious reason as to why Apple holds this event annually is 'because they can.'
If Sony could do this, they would. But Sony can't, because they don't have iPod-caliber players, or iTunes-caliber software, or an iTunes Store-caliber service.
Quittner and Lyons are either ignorant or are being disingenuous.
What gives resonance to the rumors regarding Jobs's health is the suspicion that if he were terminally ill, that this is how he'd play it, denying it until the end.

There's a difference between 2004, when Jobs and Apple kept his original cancer diagnosis a secret, and what Lyons is suggesting now, which is that Jobs is lying. The difference is that lying about this would constitute securities fraud.

What if instead of holding Tuesday's event, Apple had instead done just what Lyons and Quittner propose, and announced this week's news via press release? My guess is he would have jumped on it as evidence that Jobs was too ill to take the stage.
key_insight: The media's fixation on Jobs's health was a no-win scenario for Apple, but the legal realities of securities fraud made outright deception about a terminal diagnosis far less plausible than critics implied.
